data.title: After Sales Rebound In January, AVITA Is Set Up For A Monster Year
data.description:
Q4 In The Rear View Mirror

It’ll be pretty easy to forget the sequential sales decline in Q4 2024 if 2025 shows the consisted sequential sales growth shareholders have been hoping for. As for the February call, we got a slightly more detailed explanation of what happened in Q4 and early green shoots on Q1 2025. Specifically, management stated a few of its biggest accounts didn’t finalize pending purchases in December in order to preserve cash. This hurt sales by $3-$4 million. In January, sales normalized which should lead to a strong Q1.

AVITA’s (NASDAQ:RCEL) $18.4 million in Q4 sales missed guidance by between $3.9 million and $5.9 million. This implies most of the miss was due to the sales deferrals. At the midpoints, it leaves a $1.4 million shortfall related to fewer acute wounds in the quarter than expected. If sales had missed estimates, but still showed sequential growth, the stock’s reaction wouldn’t have been nearly as bad. The stock isn’t expensive enough to crater on a slight miss. However, the sequential decline made investors fear an adoption reversal. This ended up not happening as we can see from January’s rebound.

The stock also fell due to balance sheet worries and the OrbiMed debt deal. I’ll detail the exact changes to the OrbiMed deal later on, but I’ll mention here the trailing 12-month sales minimum in Q1 is $73 million. These new sales minimums aren’t guidance in the sense they aren’t what the firm expects to hit. However, since the minimums are meant to be sales marks the firm can comfortably hit, they do tell us what management expects. Specifically, $73 million in TTM (trailing 12-month) sales implies $19.84 million in Q1 sales. If that’ll be comfortably hit, it supports my previous projection for $21 million. In fact, results might even be slightly better than that since management said sales would be up notably from Q4.

We could easily see $22 million in sales which would flow nicely through to the bottom line since gross margins are 87.6%. I will raise my Q1 estimate to $21.5 million for accuracy’s sake. Management mentioned at the JP Morgan conference that it saw a slight benefit from the Louisiana terrorist attack and the California wildfires. I won’t add these events to my estimate, since I’m not going to review every single acute event in America every quarter. Just remember, this is an event-driven business. In a long timeframe, they are predictable. In a short timeframe, anything can happen.

With the January rebound, the weak Q4 has been put in the rearview mirror. I’m sure investors will want to see a few quarters of growth before trusting the firm completely. That starts with one good report which we will get in May. Management mentioned it has been doing 1k burn cases per month in the past 1-2 quarters and growing. With RECELL GO cartridges priced at $6.5k, that’s $19.5 million in sales per quarter. That’s not including the full-thickness wound indication. I don’t want to read too much into that comment, but at minimum it suggests the firm is at a quarterly sales run rate in the low $20s (millions).

Net Cash Used In Operations Increased As Expected

Before I get into the cash usage in the quarter, I must mention management bungled its communication on its breakeven guidance timing in its January update. Investors had been anxiously waiting for the firm to hit breakeven for a few quarters. Management pushing the target back from Q3 to Q4 hurt investor confidence. Now management is suggesting it can get to FCF positive in 2H and GAAP profitability in Q4.

There was no need to push back guidance if they think they can reach FCF positivity in Q3, since likely no investors are worried about GAAP profitability. Rather, investors are worried about the firm having enough cash to get to breakeven. The stock cratered in January because investors were confused when management said the Q4 sales decline was a one-time event, yet still pushed back the breakeven target. Personally, I loved the stock’s decline because it gave me my first chance to buy shares since I started following the company a couple of years ago.

I’ll go over management’s framing of the firm’s loss in Q4, but I must first stick with how I analyzed it last quarter to stay consistent. I can’t move from benchmark to benchmark to fit my preconceived narrative. As you can see below, AVITA’s net cash used in operations increased from $7.2 million to $8.1 million. That was around what I expected because sales fell $1.14 million sequentially. If sales increase from $18.4 million to $21.5 million in Q1, cash usage can decline to $6 million. At year end, the firm had $35.9 million in cash. In a future section, I’ll review my cash burn projections. Not to spoil things, but I think the firm can get to breakeven without needing to raise money.

Management framed the loss using different stats. Neither my analysis nor this framing are incorrect. They are just different ways of viewing the business. Let’s look at what management discussed. In Q4, AVITA used $8.5 million in cash which was down from $9.7 million in the previous quarter. This was due to the $4.1 million decline in sequential operating expenses.

On a year over year basis, operating expenses increased from $24.7 million to $26.1 million. There was a $3.9 million increase in sales and marketing expenses. This was offset by a $600k decrease in G&A and a $1.9 million decrease in R&D due to the TONE study completion. I expect sales & marketing as well as G&A expenses to remain stable in the next few quarters because the firm isn’t increasing its headcount. The ending of the TONE study is a nice tailwind, but R&D expenses from the Cohealyx 1 study will cancel that out. The firm won’t reach breakeven via cutting costs. Rather, it will gain leverage via high gross margin sales while maintaining a steady headcount.

The firm faced a cash headwind in Q4 due to accounts receivable increasing from $7.7 million last year to $11.8 million. I don’t like how management framed the sequential improvement in cash usage in relation to the year over year increase in accounts receivable. It’s better to compare sequential numbers and annual numbers separately. Accounts receivable increased from $10.3 million in Q3. That’s a valid headwind to point out since that’s a 14.6% increase in spite of a 5.8% sequential sales decline.

RECELL GO Transition & RECELL GO Mini Launch

AVITA is trying to quickly transition center usage from RECELL to RECELL GO because RECELL GO’s ease of use will lower hospitals’ burden and increase usage. RECELL GO has 1/3rd fewer steps, streamlining preparation. The goal to transition +95% of centers to RECELL GO by year-end was slightly missed. AVITA transitioned nearly all of its burn accounts to RECELL GO and transitioned over 70% of trauma centers treating full-thickness wounds. That equates to 83% of total unit volume. The transition will probably be complete in Q1. Management didn’t mention this, but I speculate some trauma centers will go directly from RECELL to RECELL GO Mini. There’s no need to use RECELL GO first. Given the need for RECELL GO Mini, I see nothing hampering the completion of the transition.

Since I follow AVITA closer than most investors, I’d like to share a confusing point I figured out along the way. The slide below shows the 398,500 RECELL-eligible procedures within the full-thickness wound market. AVITA’s management is first going after acute wounds even though RECELL GO can treat chronic wounds. This makes sense because treating a chronic wound without fixing its underlying cause won’t solve the issue permanently like it will for a wound from a traumatic event or a surgery.

Therefore, I boxed off the acute full-thickness wounds AVITA will be going after first. This confusion was spurred by me trying to forecast the potential uptick from RECELL GO Mini. This device will help AVITA go after wounds less than 2.5% of total body surface area. These small wounds represent the vast majority of the 270k non-chronic full-thickness wounds AVITA is pushing to treat.

RECELL GO Mini launched in February after the firm completed training preparations. It’s difficult to project the device’s cartridge sales in Q1 since the quarter only includes the first few weeks of uptake. Management had little sales information to go by as of the February 13th call. Mini uses the same processing device as RECELL GO. The 3 wells for the skin, buffer, and enzyme are just smaller.

Even if we knew the exact percentage of the 270k full-thickness wounds that are 2.5% or smaller, we wouldn’t know the number of doctors who were hesitant to use RECELL GO because it treats up to 10% of total body surface area. The only observations I can make are the full-thickness wound market is 1.33x the size of the burn market and the full-thickness launch has gone through fits and starts. If RECELL GO Mini effectively eases doctor’s concerns, AVITA will have accelerating revenue growth in 2025. I look forward to management breaking down sales between burns and full-thickness wounds.

Cohealyx 1 Starting In March, Early Signs Are Amazing

Personally, I am driven to invest in medical stocks due to treatment improvements versus the current standard of care. Science leads financial results. You can’t predict where the business is headed by looking at the 3 major financial statements. You must look at study data and the early experiences doctors and patients have with the devices.

This brings me to the dermal matrix called Cohealyx. It received FDA approval in December based on porcine models. AVITA went through 18 creations to settle on this dermal matrix. It has the right histological absorption so it doesn’t need to be removed, yet provides the structure for tissue and blood vessel re-growth. In full-thickness wounds, the body lacks the cells to repopulate connective tissue which leaves the wound open. The longer the wound stays open, the greater the risk of infection and scarring.

In looking at the stages of wound care, PermeaDerm can be used in the beginning and the end. In the beginning, PermeaDerm temporizes the wound. It can also be used as a dressing after RECELL is applied. PermeaDerm can be used 3-4 times in total in the wound treatment process. Getting back to Cohealyx, the dermal matrix is used to get the wound graft ready. Cohealyx re-grows connective tissue to allow the split thickness graft to take. It can be used with or without RECELL GO, increasing the addressable market.

AVITA needs IRB (Institutional Review Board) approval before it can start Cohealyx’s post-approval human study. The goal is to enroll 40 patients in 20 centers (study called Cohealyx 1). There will be a 26-week measurement period, but AVITA doesn’t need to wait that long to use the data in Cohealyx’ commercialization. The study was designed so AVITA can use the data as it comes out. Since Cohealyx can get wounds graft ready in just 7 days, it’ll have enough complete responses to get centers to try it quickly after the study starts. The goal is to commence commercialization on April 1st.

Cohealyx has been used on a few patients prior to formal enrollment of Cohealyx 1. The images below are from the first patient treated at The Ohio State Wexner Medical Center. The wound was graft ready after 7 days just like in the porcine models. None of the pre-study wounds have taken longer than 7 days to be graft ready. According to the article posted on The Ohio State website, the current standard of care gets wounds graft ready after 2-4 weeks. The first patient wasn’t treated with RECELL after Cohealyx (study enrolls patients with and without RECELL). Just by using Cohealyx, the patient’s hospital stay was cut from what would have been 1 month, to just 10 days. Cohealyx’s ease of use in the operating room could allow more patients to be treated.

Management showed Cohealyx to 50 physicians and they all wanted to try it. It’s easy to understand why because the potential advantages are straightforward. To spur adoption, AVITA will price the dermal matrix at a 20% discount to the competition. The TAM calculations management has shown include this discounted price. Besides improved time to graft and a lower price, AVITA will sell Cohealyx via a consignment model with RFID tracking for better inventory management. Consignment is when the sale only occurs when the dermal matrix is used. AVITA will store Cohealyx at the centers. With all these advantages, I expect Cohealyx to get through Value Analysis Committees (VACs) quickly. Cohealyx’s attach rate to RECELL GO should increase rapidly over the next few quarters.

My New Sales Estimate & Valuation Target

As I mentioned, I think $21.5 million in sales in Q1 is reasonable. Management mentioned PermeaDerm is finally gaining momentum and RECELL GO sales rebounded after the weak Q4. It’s difficult to model much sales from RECELL GO Mini in Q1 since it launched in February. In Q2, I foresee $24.5 million in sales due to expanded usage of RECELL GO Mini in the full-thickness wound indication and early usage of Cohealyx. I expect a bigger increase in Cohealyx usage starting in 2H which is why I project $29.5 million and $31.5 million in sales in Q3 and Q4.

If you compare this to my last estimate, I shifted sales slightly from Q2 to Q3 to allow Cohealyx more time to gain adoption. I became more optimistic on 2nd half sales due to my increased confidence in Cohealyx. This equates to $107 million in 2025 sales which is slightly above management’s guidance high-end of $106 million and above my previous estimate of $103.5 million.

I will detail how various PermeaDerm/Cohealyx attach rates can impact sales in the next few quarters. As I mentioned, 1,000 burns treated per month equates to $19.5 million in RECELL cartridge sales. A 100% attach rate at the low-end of the listed prices of PermeaDerm and Cohealyx equates to $6 million and $60 million in sales per quarter. Remember, this doesn’t include full-thickness wounds which can be treated with all products. Furthermore, PermeaDerm can be used 3-4 times per wound and Cohealyx can be used without RECELL GO. Also, burns treated per month will increase.

A 10% attach rate equates to $600k and $6 million in sales per quarter for PermeaDerm and Cohealyx. If in 2025 the firm gets both to a 10% attach rate, burn treatments increase, and RECELL GO Mini increases market share in full-thickness wounds, my estimate will be too low.

If AVITA trades at 10x sales, that equates to a $1.07 billion market cap. As I will get to next, AVITA issued warrants to adjust the OrbiMed debt deal. The firm ended 2024 with 26,146,234 shares outstanding. I’ll conservatively estimate the firm will end 2025 with 27 million shares outstanding. This gives me a price target of $39.63. The stock is below my price target because of worries about RECELL GO adoption due to the 2 sales misses in 2024; the lack of proven adoption of its new products including RECELL GO Mini, PermeaDerm, and Cohealyx; and worries about the cash burn/balance sheet which I’ll review next.

OrbiMed Deal Amended Again, Cash Burn Estimate

For the 4th time, AVITA amended its credit agreement with OrbiMed. In exchange for lowering the sales thresholds it needs to hit, AVITA issued warrants to OrbiMed in which it can purchase the equivalent of 145,180 shares of stock. Besides the Q1 threshold of $73 million, the firm needs to hit TTM sales of $78 million, $84 million, $92 million, and $103 million in the following 4 quarters. Each quarter subsequently needs to have TTM sales of $115 million. As you can see, the firm needs to have $92 million in sales this year which is decently below the current guidance range of $100-$106 million.

My cash burn prediction per quarter no longer includes the $2 million in quarterly repayments to OrbiMed because AVITA no longer will violate the sales minimums that would cause it to repay the $40 million loan. I tweaked my cash burn projections due to changes in my sales estimates. I think the firm will only burn another $10 million before getting to breakeven. This means its $35.9 million in cash is more than enough to get there. Once the market realizes this, the stock will rise. I was able to add to my investment position after the close on Thursday before the stock increased because I believe few investors are paying as close attention to AVITA as I am.

No Details On Trauma Centers VACs On The Call, CE Mark Delay

Management didn’t detail the number of VAC acceptances in the full-thickness wound indication for RECELL GO like they typically have in recent quarters. Management forecasted this change on the previous call. While I’m fine with this, I wish new information was added on adoption rates. Each center doesn’t do the same treatment volume, so telling investors about individual additions doesn’t give us much color on the firm’s near-term growth rate.

If the firm gives new details on its February 18th webinar, I’ll mention it in the comment section. Usually there isn’t new information in them, but they give color on the facts already provided. Just because the VAC numbers weren’t disclosed doesn’t mean approvals aren’t a risk to the future growth cadence. It's important to be cautious about the timing of future approvals given the history of delays. I think VACs will be easier to achieve with PermeaDerm and Cohealyx because they don’t define a new category of devices like RECELL does.

Management now expects RECELL GO to get the CE Mark in the middle of 2025. That’s 3 months later than they said on the last call. It has been pushed back a few times. Good thing management didn’t expect much sales from Europe, Australia, and New Zealand in 2025. I’m not surprised it has taken so long because Stereotaxis’ (STXS) MAGiC CE Mark took 8 months longer than I originally expected. America will be the primary focus for AVITA in the next few years anyway.

One final new risk is the business is more event-driven than expected. While we have a good idea of how many burns and full-thickness wounds there will be each year, occasionally some quarters have more or less than expected given the nature of acute wound events. Investors will stress less about minor sales hiccups once the firm hits breakeven.

data.title: ABN AMRO: Still Cheap At 7x Earnings; Possible 10% Total Yield
data.description:
Dutch bank ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) (OTCPK:ABMRF) closed out 2024 on a positive note, with fourth quarter results coming in stronger than analysts expected.

Having risen around 8% on the day, ABN AMRO is now trading at a five-year high in its underlying currency, albeit the dollar-denominated ADSs are a bit lower due to FX. This has also powered a decent performance since my last update in November, with ADSs returning around 11% in that time.

Since I first covered it, ABN AMRO has basically been a classic value stock. Specifically, its steep discount to tangible book value, reasonable return on tangible equity, and ability to distribute most of its earnings to shareholders has supported an ongoing double-digit shareholder yield. While timing on capital returns has been a slight issue recently, this hasn't really altered the underlying investment case.

Looking ahead, FY2025 guidance could support a slightly better year than pre-results consensus had penciled in – with the latter already mapping to an attractive-looking P/E of ~7x and a shareholder yield of ~10%. Although I am lowering the stock a notch due to its performance since my last update, ABN AMRO continues to look cheap, and I attach a 'Buy' rating to the shares.

Solid Q4 Results & Guidance

For its fourth quarter, ABN AMRO reported pre-tax profit of €619 million, beating consensus by just over 3%. Annualizing the fourth quarter result wouldn't be particularly useful due to seasonal effects. However, full-year net income attributable to ordinary shareholders was €2.25 billion – better than the ~€2 billion I thought the bank would earn a year ago. This mapped to a return on tangible equity of roughly 10%.

The fourth quarter beat was arguably stronger than the headline figure looks. Positively, revenue of €2.24 billion was around 3% ahead of consensus. Breaking this down, net interest income of €1.67 billion beat consensus by over 4%, while fee and commission income also came in stronger than expected, with the €500 million print beating consensus by almost €30 million. This was driven by higher mortgage advisory fees as the Dutch mortgage market continued its recovery and by increased transaction volumes in the bank's clearing business.

Costs were a bit more mixed. Operating expenses of €1.61 billion missed by nearly 9%, though again this includes one-off items. Underlying operating expenses were around €1.50 billion, missing by less than 1%.

Credit costs were a circa €80 million beat. Although this is a volatile line (and therefore hard to forecast quarter-to-quarter), resilient asset quality has been an ongoing theme among the European banks I have covered this quarter, so I view this as a positive, especially given upbeat guidance (more on that below).

Looking ahead, I think the FY2025 outlook looks reasonably healthy as well. Starting with revenue, management expects the bank to generate roughly €6.3 billion in NII this year, giving a range of €6.2 billion to €6.4 billion. This would be around 3% below the FY2024 print (€6.5 billion) and around 6% below the fourth quarter run rate, with management expecting a modest headwind from the bank's interest rate hedging program.

Importantly, FY2025 guidance would still leave NII around 16% above 2022 and 22% above 2021, meaning the bank will retain most of the tailwind it received from normalizing interest rates.

Breaking things down a little further, management expects higher volumes to be offset by lower lending margins – as lower LTV ratios in Dutch mortgages slightly compresses the credit risk premium on loan yields. Positively, the Dutch mortgage market continues to rebound, with house prices and transaction volumes both up nicely last year, and management expects this to continue in FY2025. This is important as domestic mortgages account for around 60% of ABN AMRO's total loan book.

The lower-end of NII guidance assumes no change to customer deposit rates, which is probably conservative given that interest rates are falling in the Eurozone. Deposit trends looked positive at the end of last year, with the bank seeing a mix shift from term balances back into demand and current accounts.

Moreover, guidance also excludes the acquisition of German private bank Hauck Aufhäuser Lampe ('HAL'). Assuming that deal closes as planned in H1, this will contribute a small amount to full-year NII. Weighing all that up, I think there is probably more upside than downside compared to where FY2025 consensus was before results (~€6.32 billion).

I would say the same with respect to fee & commission income. Compared to pre-results FY2024 consensus of €1.88 billion, analysts saw this line rising by around 4% to €1.96 billion. Not only did ABN AMRO beat FY2024 estimates on this line by just over €20 million, but management also expects FY2025 fee income growth to be "above ambition" relative to its 3%-5% medium-term target CAGR. HAL will contribute, while the outlook for mortgage advisory fees and the bank's wealth and asset services businesses all seem positive.

Costs are arguably a more speculative source of upside. Management's outlook for flat operating expenses (~€5.3 billion) would be better than consensus. Although there are levers to pull here (some projects are winding down and external employee count is falling), operating costs have generally been a soft spot for the bank – so I wouldn't be inclined to take this at face value.

On the flip side, credit loss provisioning has been more fruitful recently. Management sees that continuing this year, with FY2025 impairment charges once again seen landing below the bank's 15-20bps through-the-cycle yardstick. Compared to pre-results consensus of 16bps, this could be a source of support for the bottom line, as was the case in recent years. I would note that ABN AMRO's domestic mortgage book has a sub-55% blended LTV, giving it plenty of scope to avoid losses.

Shares At 7x Forward P/E; Potential 10% Total Yield

While I think the above might lead to some upside to FY2025 earnings, these shares still look cheap either way, with the current €16.95 share price (~$17.70 per ADS) equating to 6.2x FY2024 EPS (€2.72 per share), 7.1x pre-results FY2025 consensus EPS (€2.39 per share), and ~0.63x year-end tangible book value per share (€26.86 per share).

Given ABN AMRO aims to pay out 50% of net income via dividends, this also implies a yield of ~8% based on the FY2024 DPS and ~7% based on the prospective FY2025 payout.

In recent years, the bank has supplemented this with €500 million in annual share buybacks, which has usually been confirmed following fourth quarter results. I mentioned last time that this year's announcement has been delayed to Q2 FY2025, as the bank was still seeking clarity on the impact of Basel IV among other issues.

There is still nothing to suggest that this is anything other than a timing problem. Earnings remain relatively robust, and given the bank's regulatory capital ratio remains a little above management's target, there could even be some upside to that figure.

Taking the prospective dividend allocation (~€970 million), buybacks (~€500 million) and the bank's current market-cap (~€14.1 billion), the total yield in respect of FY2025 would be just over 10%.

Conclusion

ABN AMRO ended 2024 with a pretty upbeat set of fourth quarter figures, beating consensus. Looking ahead, while the benefits of higher interest rates have now played out, guidance surrounding fee income and costs can still support robust FY2025 earnings. Based on the current stock price, that could set investors up for a ~10% forward shareholder yield on a single-digit P/E ratio. Accordingly, I remain bullish on ABN AMRO, rating its shares a 'Buy'.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Why I Took A Position And Believe It Will Move Higher
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) isn't the typical company I would normally invest in, but I took a position that consists of shares and options. In full transparency, my cost basis on the shares is $33.62, while I purchased calls at a $35 strike that expires on 2/28 as a short-term play. I also wrote spreads where I purchased $32 calls expiring on 3/21 and wrote the $50 calls against them as a hedge. SMCI is down -46.51% over the past year and is very far away from its 52-week high of $122.90. Back in 2020, the SEC charged SMCI and its former CFO with accounting violations, and in August 2024, Seeking Alpha reported on a new short report from Hindenburg Research that discussed similar ideas. This isn't normally my cup of tea, but SMCI is generating real revenue and real profit, as its products are critical to the A.I. build-out. On 2/11, SMCI delivered their preliminary Q2 2025 results, and when I dug through the numbers, this investment looked like a risk worth taking for me. I believe there is an opportunity for a large ROI in both the short and long term with SMCI, and this is a calculated risk I am taking.

Risks to investing in SMCI

Investing in SMCI comes with risk factors that everyone should be aware of. Investors should do their own research because SMCI could very easily retrace back down to its 52-week lows. SMCI hasn't delivered its 2024 10-K and didn't even report its official numbers for Q2 2025. I am willing to take a chance on SMCI because the potential outweighs the risks for me, which may not be the case for others. SMCI operates in a competitive market and competes against companies like Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE), and Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG). SMCI may not keep up with cutting-edge technology which could make their servers, storage systems, and liquid cooling equipment inferior to other products which could negatively impact revenue. There is also a chance that the CapEx spending on A.I. infrastructure will slow down, and if it does, fewer of SMCI's products will be needed. SMCI is not a sure thing, and there is a lot more risk regarding this investment than other companies I write about.

Why I bought a mixture of call options and shares

I normally write covered calls against my positions to generate income, but in this case, I decided to purchase call options and call spreads on SMCI in addition to shares. I purchased the shares because I feel the company is undervalued, and as it moves higher, I plan on writing covered calls against my position to manufacture income. I purchased naked calls that expire on 2/28 because NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) reports on Wednesday 2/26, and I believe they are going to beat on the top and bottom line due to the amount of capital that was spent on CapEx in Q4 by Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META). If NVDA comes out with a big beat and strong guidance, I believe it will become a tide that lifts all ships and drags SMCI higher. If SMCI delivers the 10-K for 2024 and their new accounting firm issues an all-clear statement, that could be another short-term catalyst, and this needs to happen before 2/25 otherwise, SMCI faces getting delisted. I also purchased call spreads, which means I bought calls at a $32 strike price expiring on 3/21 and sold the $50 calls against it to lower my cost basis. I did this because they were relatively inexpensive, and I got an extra month before they expire. The calls and call spreads are my short-term investments on SMCI, and if things go well over the next several weeks, the shares I purchased will likely become a longer-term investment position in the portfolio.

Why I am getting bullish after the preliminary Q2 report

On the earnings call, Michael Staiger, the VP of Investor Relations and Corporate Development, started by saying SMCI would not address questions regarding the delay in the 2024 10-K or the quarterly 10-Q that were due. Directly after his opening remarks, Charles Liang (SMCI's CEO) disclosed that SMCI's finance team and BDO, who is their new auditor, have been engaged in completing the outstanding audit. He provided some clarity on what to expect and indicated that based on the work that has taken place, all outstanding documents should be filed by the deadline of 2/25/25. SMCI has filled senior-level positions across its communication, operations, finance, legal, and compliance departments over the past two quarters and is in the process of hiring a new CFO and CCO. Mr. Liang also emphasized that the special committee that was put in place found no evidence to support the previous auditing firm resigning from their engagement. If SMCI can deliver its 2024 10-K and other required files by the 25th and gets a clean bill of health from BDO, I think its share price will react favorably to the news.

Meta Platforms, Inc. ((META), Alphabet Inc. ((GOOG)) (GOOGL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) allocated just over $217 billion toward their CapEx budgets in 2024. On the recent earnings calls from AMZN, MSFT, GOOGL, and META, we learned that none of these companies are taking their foot off the gas, and they will collectively be spending $320 billion on CapEx during the 2025 fiscal year. This is an increase of $102.73 billion YoY, and MSFT is already halfway through its fiscal year, where they plan on spending $80 billion on CapEx for A.I. and data centers. AMZN came over the top and announced their plans to spend over $100 billion on CapEx, which makes me believe that on the 2025 annual call this summer from MSFT, they will have no choice but to either maintain their current CapEx budget or increase it to compete with AMZN. This could also lead GOOGL and META to increase their CapEx spending in 2026, which is very bullish for NVDA and SMCI. Outside of the big 4, Oracle Corporation (ORCL) discussed on their Q2 earnings call that they needed to expand 66 of their current data centers and build an additional 100 data centers due to their contracted demand. If none of these companies are slowing down, then we should see increased levels of NVDA chips being shipped while more infrastructure spending is allocated toward SMCI.

SMCI has several key opportunities that can drive its forward growth. SMCI's partnership with NVDA is critical as they have already begun shipping air-cooled and liquid-cooled systems for NVIDIA Blackwell products. We learned on the conference call that volume shipments have already commenced for air-cooled 10U and liquid-cooled 4U NVIDIA B200 HGX systems, while NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 racks are operational. SMCI has been a choice partner of NVDA, and this allows them to have a first-to-market advantage, which could reinforce SMCI's revenue projections as they are a critical provider of infrastructure equipment throughout the data center space. SMCI expanded its total liquid-cooled data center infrastructure solutions as it shipped more than 3,000 DLC racks in 2024, which comprised roughly 70% of the DLC market. SMCI also offers customers on-site deployment, cabling, and servicing, which can provide additional revenue and be a critical reason why companies choose SMCI over someone else.

The preliminary Q2 earnings data was very promising as SMCI finished 2024 generating $14.94 billion in revenue, and they are projecting that they will end the 2025 fiscal year generating between $23.5 billion and $25 billion. This would be a YoY increase of $8.56 billion or 57.27% from the amount of revenue generated in the 2024 fiscal year if SMCI delivers on the low end of its guidance. They also indicated that they are paving the way to generate $40 billion in revenue during the 2026 fiscal year, which starts in July. This is very bullish to me, considering they are expecting to increase revenue by 57.27% this year and potentially 70.21% next year.

SMCI generated $14.94 billion in revenue and $1.21 billion in net income during their 2024 fiscal year, which puts their profit margin at 8.08%. Assuming that their margins stay the same and SMCI comes in at the low end of their 2025 guidance, they would generate $23.5 billion in revenue and $1.9 billion in net income. Based on their fully diluted share count of approximately 642 million shares on a GAAP accounting basis, SMCI could potentially generate $2.96 of EPS for the 2025 fiscal year. This would be a YoY increase of $0.79 or 36.37% compared to the $2.17 of EPS SMCI generated in 2024. The analyst community is only expecting SMCI to generate $2.62 of EPS for the 2025 fiscal year and then grow EPS by 42.94% in the 2026 fiscal year to $3.74. The analyst community is only expecting $33.2 billion of revenue in 2026, which is well below the vision SMCI provided in their remarks. SMCI looks inexpensive based on the analyst expectations as it is trading at 15.16 times 2025 earnings and 10.6 times 2026 earnings.

Conclusion

SMCI has a fair amount of risk associated with it, but this could break out hard to the upside in the next 2-3 weeks. We have already seen a strong upward move as shares have appreciated roughly 40% over the past month. Based on the numbers, I think SMCI is a risk worth taking because if NVDA beats earnings and delivers strong guidance, it would help solidify SMCI's guidance. SMCI's short interest rate is also 13.17%, and after the recent move, they may need to start covering. SMCI has a lot of potential growth on the horizon and trades at a multiple that seems depressed for the amount of revenue and profitability they're able to generate. When I look at the CapEx spending across big-tech and NVDA's revenue growth, I think that SMCI will experience significant revenue growth over the next 2 years. I wouldn't be surprised if shares of SMCI exceed $50 by the end of February as long as they get a clean bill of health from BDO.

data.title: Powerfleet: A Great Story, But I'll Pass
data.description:
Investment Thesis

PowerFleet, Inc. (NASDAQ:AIOT) has been on a tear this past year, with the stock up more than 160% in 12 months.

Naturally, with the stock rapidly moving higher, many investors are excited about this relatively small-cap business, with a market cap of $1 billion, with an alluring narrative, at the right time.

Basically, this company describes itself as a IoT opportunity for vehicles and warehousing operations.

However, beyond its narrative, I see a couple of blemishes that keep on the sidelines. As such, I'll have to stick to the sidelines and cheer the bulls on from the sidelines.

Powerfleet's Near-Term Prospects

Powerfleet provides a platform where customers can manage their vehicles, and warehouse operations using smart IOT technology.

They provide AI and IoT-driven solutions helping companies improve productivity and reduce risks.

With their warehousing platform, Unity, Powerfleets seeks to become the go-to platform for monitoring fleets and in-warehouse operations, giving customers real-time insights about their vehicles.

Further, during their fiscal Q3 2025 earnings call, they describe their large customers being spread into beverages companies, utilities, and mining, clearly Powerfleet's operations are nicely diversified.

Moreover, for fiscal 2026, starting in April 2025, Powerfleet has big ambitions for its AI-powered cameras and warehouse solutions, as well as partnering up with telco companies to further accelerate their growth.

Meanwhile, Powerfleet is looking to exit lower-growth areas, like an old electronic logging device business, to enable management to fully focus on higher-value opportunities.

As I said already, there's a lot to be excited about its narrative. And now, let's move beyond its narrative so we can discuss its fundamentals.

AIOT Revenue Growth Rates Require Interpretation

AIOT's revenue growth rates on financial outlets, such as SA, don't match up with the company's own reporting. What you see in the graphic above are the growth rates taken from the company's press releases.

On top of that, fiscal Q3 2025 marks the first full quarter since the acquisition of Fleet Complete and the third full quarter since closing the business combination with MiX Telematics Ltd.

So, when we look at the outlook for fiscal Q4 2025 when the company is expecting to grow by more than 200% y/y, it isn't that AIOT has suddenly become a world-class, IOT SaaS company. Rather, it is that the accounting has been skewed by a couple of back to back acquisitions.

In fact, to be clear, management notes on the earnings call that fiscal 2025 is expected to be up around 7% to 10% y/y. This is an increase from its previously announced 5% topline growth for fiscal 2025, but nothing in the same ballpark as the triple digits that it appears to be growing if we just take its $365 million revenues guidance at face value.

With that in mind, let's now discuss its valuation.

AIOT Stock Valuation -- 3x Sales

As an Inflection investor, I will typically seek debt-free businesses, with around 5% of their market caps made up of cash. In the case of AIOT, it carries about $235 million of net debt.

In simple terms, more than 20% of the company’s market cap consists of debt, which I find excessive. Even if the company achieves remarkable growth and success, I won’t feel comfortable underwriting its future prospects with that level of debt.

Moreover, the business is guiding for about $80 million of EBITDA this year.

But consider this, out of the $32 million of adjusted EBITDA reported in the first 9 months of 2024, this translated into $6 million of free cash flow burn. Put in other words, there's a large drag between the amount of EBITDA and its free cash flow.

So, yes, the company is clearly on a highly acquisitive path. However, with approximately $35 million of debt being made current, meaning debt that's due in the next 12 months, and less than $34 million of cash and cash equivalents on its books, I see a problem.

Further Risk Factors to Consider

AIOT describes itself as a business with a significant opportunity to re-rate higher.

The idea being floated by management is that they trade at just 2.5x sales (now closer to 3x sales), while Samsara (IOT) is priced at 20x sales. Since Deep Value Returns is long Samsara, I believe I'm in a good place to make the following comment.

Samsara is delivering $1 billion in sales, with clean and growing free cash flow, and growing at approximately 30% CAGR. Also, since I own Samsara, you should naturally expect its balance sheet to be mighty strong, as it, in fact, is, as it operates debt-free.

The other risk factor that I'm wary of is represented in the graphic that follows:

As you can see above, about 25% of its revenues come from Africa. Do I really want to pay a heavy premium for a business with so much African exposure? Personally, I don't find that too compelling.

The Bottom Line

Powerfleet has an exciting narrative, but when I dig into the financials, I see more risk than reward.

The company carries significant debt, with more than 20% of its market cap tied up in net debt. Moreover, its free cash flow remains negative despite reporting positive adjusted EBITDA.

While revenue growth looks impressive at first glance, much of it is driven by acquisitions rather than organic expansion.

On top of that, with a substantial portion of revenue coming from Africa, I question whether it deserves a premium valuation. Basically, this isn't the next Samsara.

While some investors may be excited about PowerFleet, Inc.'s potential, I see too many yellow flags and will be sitting this one out. Good luck bulls, I'll cheer you on from the sidelines.

data.title: EUR Money Market Faces Gradual Tightening From ECB's Shrinking Balance Sheet
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder

ECB to continue cutting rates, possibly more than markets are pricing

Our economists still think the European Central Bank (ECB) could end this cycle with a terminal rate at 1.75%, ending up in slightly accommodative territory compared to neutral interest rate estimates ranging mostly from 1.75% to 2.25%. We think that, in the near term, a combination of adverse headlines and data disappointments could push market pricing temporarily even closer to a terminal rate of 1.5%.

Markets are only now starting to lean in the direction of our economists’ prediction given the growing list of downside risks to the economic outlook. Overall macro indicators continue to show weakness. January’s composite PMI barely made it above the boom-or-bust level, while the manufacturing PMIs have remained mired in contractionary territory for more than two years now. The prospect for fiscal stimulus also looks distant. There might be some stimulus coming through out of Germany after the elections, but several countries are under excessive deficit procedures, forcing them to maintain tighter budgets. Our economists anticipate stagnation over the winter months, followed by a modest recovery, resulting in only 0.7% GDP growth in 2025.

At the same time, the ECB seems more confident of reaching its inflation goals in line with forecasts, even as monthly inflation prints are showing ticks higher for the fourth time since headline inflation normalised from record highs. We sense that a growing number of officials have become more geared towards supporting growth. Indeed we think the ECB will have to be more nimble amid the influence of policies abroad, which might leave it picking up the pieces as domestic politics remain largely incapacitated.

The ECB is in rate cutting mode

Excess liquidity will remain ample in 2025 despite the ECB's balance sheet unwind

Looking at the operational aspects of monetary policy implementation, one of the ECB's main goals at the moment is the normalisation of its balance sheet. The central bank wants to have less of a footprint in overall markets outside of setting interest rates. At least part of the reason for this is regaining policy space and “restocking” the tool box.

By now, all targeted liquidity refinancing operations (TLTROs) are matured, but the ECB's asset portfolio still holds €4.3tr in bonds, contributing to around €3tr of excess reserves in the banking system. €510bn will roll off over the course of 2025 and will – all else equal – reduce excess liquidity by the same amount.

The overall sense is that the banking system is still operating at levels of excess reserves well above those that would trigger a stronger rise in money market rates and spreads. That level, depending on literature, is estimated to be around €1.5tr to €2tr. Approaching that level, the expectation is that the system will then start to rely more on the ECB again as a provider of liquidity via its open market operations, offsetting the impact of quantitative tightening and stabilising the overall level of reserves in the system.

ECB balance sheet is still to decline over the next years

Banks' demand for reserves will determine when the point of market tightness is reached

By the end of the year, excess reserves in the banking system are expected to have reached a level of around €2.5tr. The exact level from where on money market conditions would start to tighten and impact spreads to a greater degree, will largely depend on the desired level of cash reserves banks want to operate with.

Liquidity is not only needed to fulfil the minimum reserve requirement set by the ECB – by definition NOT as part of the excess reserves – but beyond that, demand is also determined by operational needs and buffers, e.g., anticipated cash flow settlements. Another important role is played by regulatory requirements where cash reserves can form part of the high quality liquid assets (HQLA) portfolio to meet the liquidity coverage ratio (LCR, which requires banks to hold a sufficient stock of high-quality liquid assets, HQLA, that can be converted into cash easily and immediately to survive a period of significant liquidity stress lasting 30 calendar days).

Banks' LCR ratios remain relatively high overall all at 159% in third quarter 2024, according to ECB data for the weighted average of “significant institutions”. Any reduction of this ratio is likely to be only very gradual, not least due to the signalling effect and peer pressure. For now, banks have also been able to cover the need for cash reserves with other sources of market funding, also via unsecured commercial paper, bond or covered bond issuance.

Banks have already increased their market funding as the ECB withdraws

This implies HQLA portfolios are remaining larger for now, but within those portfolios banks can, of course, substitute declining reserves with high quality bonds. And they have indeed done so when the TLTROs matured and excess liquidity levels declined, the added incentive now being that bonds have a higher return. With (ASW) curves steepening, bonds could become even more attractive in relative terms. But then also credit risks and limits to the amount, for example, of sovereign bonds a bank may hold have to be kept in mind.

In theory these bond holdings can also always serve as collateral to be used in the collateralised ECB liquidity operations (the weekly MRO and three-month LTROs) to convert them to cash reserves, especially when the price differential between market repo and the MRO rate is very small. The ECB had lowered the MRO rate to 15bp above the deposit facility rate last December to make the ECB’s operations more attractive and, in turn, also a more effective back-stop for the money market in general.

Banks' recourse to ECB liquidity operations remains relatively low

But a lingering stigma effect appears to still surround the ECB operations. No bank would want to be seen as having to resort to these operations in larger size. It could be interpreted as not having access to regular market funding to cover cash needs. The relative attractiveness to market rates will likely have to grow in order to draw in wider participation to overcome the perceived “first-mover disadvantage”.

For now, banks' liquidity take-up in the ECB's MROs has been muted and is mainly attributable to banks in Italy, and to a lesser degree (likely smaller) banks in Germany and Greece. More generally, one would expect to witness a more noticeable pickup first in jurisdictions where the excess reserves cushion above the minimum requirement is relatively low.

Excess liquidity is not distributed evenly in the banking system

Market segmentation contributes to unsecured rates staying below secured rates

For now, the impact on spreads stemming from the ECB’s shrinking balance sheet has been mainly felt in the realm of secured transactions. Rates on repurchase agreements (‘repo’) were feeling the largest impact and have risen versus €STR and OIS, mainly as collateral has become more abundant – not just as ECB holdings roll off, but also as the net funding needs of issuers remain at elevated levels.

We are at a point where the ECB facilities are starting to cap or at least noticeably slow the dynamics. Repo rates are now in the range of just 1bp below (German collateral) to 2bp above (Italian collateral) the ECB’s deposit facility rate. The intuition is that at repo rates above the deposit facility rate banks should start shifting out of ECB deposits and place cash into market reverse repos, earning the additional interest.

Repo rates have moved towards the ECB’s depo rate, but €STR lags noticeably

In theory, non-banks could also shift out of bank deposits into repos. Ultimately it could be one channel to cheapen also the unsecured overnight rate (€STR) relative to the deposit facility rate. However, €STR has remained more anchored and still sits around 8bp below the deposit facility rate, having moved upwards only very marginally. Counterintuitively, that means the unsecured rate is well below the secured rate. The ECB’s Isabel Schnabel has discussed several factors contributing to this effect:

For one, the short-term funding market is still very segmented. On the lending side many participants are only active in either one of the markets, repo or €STR, and very few are present in both. The hurdles to participating in the (cleared) repo market remain especially high. For banks themselves, though, repo markets are more attractive due to regulatory reasons where secured lending is treated preferentially. Also on the borrowing side, central clearing of repos allows for the netting of borrowing and lending which can also reduce regulatory/balance sheet cost.

The result is that the unsecured market is mainly used to channel excess liquidity from non-bank(s) to the ECB via the banks that have access to the central bank facilities – with banks applying the charges for the balance sheet costs in the form of a negative depo spread.

Money market funds have seen inflows alongside inverted rate curves

For now, there is no end in sight for non-banks needing to place cash in the deposit market, from money market funds, for instance, that only make use of repos to some extent due to intra-day liquidity needs. The interest rate curve is likely to stay inverted, directing flows towards money market funds – that is at least until summer when the ECB comes close to the end of its cutting cycle. But even thereafter, a notable resteepening is not ensured. At the same time that also means even smaller banks without repo access do not feel a greater need to borrow with liquidity conditions still comfortable.

The upward pressure on unsecured rates may remain muted in the near term. If it were to rise then it should likely be felt in rising Euribor-OIS spreads first, i.e., in the terms beyond one month that have more regulatory value for banks.

Unsecured rates have yet to feel more upside pressure

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: CONY: 150% Yield Not Enough To Buy This Coinbase ETF
data.description:
When I see a 150% yield on an ETF, my first thought is simply "NO." No way it is for real, no way I'm investing in it, no way it will maintain that yield, even if it is real. As it turns out, when it comes to YieldMax COIN Option Income Strategy (NYSEARCA:CONY), one of a few dozen ETFs from that firm, and the one that applies its unique option-infused approach to the stock of Coinbase Global (COIN), the answers are as follows:

  • Yes, that 150% dividend yield did happen. But it would take a perfect storm in a positive way for that to happen again.
  • I'm still a "no" on CONY, though if I were a very aggressive investor, it would interest me more. As it is, the underlying stock is so volatile, I can't pursue CONY using my preferred position setup, by adding a protective put option contract (on COIN) alongside it, thus defining my worst-case scenario before I see what happens to the stock and ETF.
  • No, I don't think CONY can replicate that past performance, which relies on a combination of COIN stock appreciating and volatility of the stock staying as high as it has been.

But I suspect that won't stop some investors from falling for the "sticker envy" of a 150% dividend yield. After all, just putting 1/10 of a portfolio in something that actually yields 150%, and 90% in T-bills would produce a nearly 20% overall portfolio yield. And if I had done that the past 12 months, I might have approached that. So we can add this to the long history of great "wins" I never attained. That's the life of a risk-managed investor, I suppose.

CONY: About that 150% yield

Here's what the Seeking Alpha profile page on CONY shows. 150%. That's the simple calculation that divides total dividend distributions per share of the past 12 months into the price of CONY. That's an all-star performance over the trailing 12 months, even if it is unlikely to be repeated.

Yet, the fund's own page for CONY has the yield at 3/4 of that shown above. And the 30-day SEC Yield, which is essentially the yield for the past month's payout, is 3.5%. That implies a 42% yield. What's going on here?

ETF dividend math, explained

CONY is a great example of how not to get fooled by the way the SEC regulates how dividend yields on ETFs are reported. Some funds, like most stocks, do not see their payout gyrate wildly from quarter to quarter. But with YieldMax ETFs, that can easily happen. That's because the yield distributed each month is not at all based on COIN's dividend. It doesn't pay one.

Instead, the monthly dividend is a distribution of most of the ETF's return from a coordinated set of option transactions. One options pair essentially replicates owning COIN, the underlying stock here. The others are covered call writing positions on that replicated stock position.

Normally with this ETF family, I just show the 4 or 5 options positions in my articles. But with CONY, the combination of frenetic price movement in the underlying stock (leading to a volatility level implied by COIN options of around 60-70%, versus around 15% for the S&P 500), and asset flows with the ETF itself, have made the managers of this active fund busier than usual.

There's an important investor lesson here. I am confident there are investors who looked at CONY a year ago and decided not to buy it because "the expense ratio is too high." As I've noted for years, expense ratio with ETFs is something I stay aware of, but rarely does it stop me from proceeding if I believe the ETF's total return over my desired holding period can make that a relatively small cost of holding it. Too many investors obsess over this aspect of investing.

If this was an S&P 500 ETF, I get it. But it is an ETF that does this:

I do a lot of options trading, but even I don't want to bother with all of that work. That's part of why one would pay for the managers here to orchestrate that triple-digit yield. Even if it is not sustainable, CONY ought to continue yielding comfortably in the double digits. But likely a lot closer to the 42% cited above than 150%. And while even the lower figure sounds like a slam dunk, the risk of price declines going forward leads me to believe that the best days for CONY are behind it for a while.

That's a bi-product of a stock that flew higher over the past year, then stalled and became very "whippy" in price, once the fever pitch post-spot Bitcoin ETF launches and the run for that leading cryptocurrency to $100,000 set in.

As a reminder, the expectation on YieldMax ETFs is that over time, it will capture about 80% of the upside and downside of the underlying stock.

To finish the yield math, note that the yield we typically see (except for that SEC 30-day yield) is the last 12 payments. So for now, that's the two in 2025 and the last 10 in 2024. I included the last payment of 2023 here, so we can see that the $2.46 and $2.69 payments have already dropped off. And as of July, all but one of the distributions of $1.50 or higher will be off the 12-month trailing tally.

And while that alone is not a reason to avoid this ETF, it will likely surprise some folks when this yield chart takes a dive.

CONY: why I'm not jumping in now

OK, so that's the mechanics of CONY and that remarkable, but factually accurate yield... from the past. Going forward, my goal is to determine if I want to buy CONY. And I have decided to refrain from doing so at this point, for this reason. While the stock does not look awful technically, it is still way too volatile to own it the way I want. That is, by pairing CONY with a put option on COIN, to sacrifice some total return and yield, but to cap my downside.

The ETF has a short history, but it has already produced periods in which it lost nearly 40% in three months, albeit with quick recoveries. But the debut of crypto spot ETFs, and the surge in interest in Bitcoin and others, while still in position to get more popular, may have moved past its initial "number go up" phase. Regardless those puts are way too expensive.

COIN: the business is one I'm watching

The good news, however, is that now I can start to look longer-term at the possibility of coming back to CONY if and when it starts to mature as a business. If it reaches that point, I can put it in the classification of how I use YieldMax ETFs to "own" some of the Magnificent 7 stocks. With put protection alongside in most cases.

COIN has certainly been a strong proxy for higher crypto prices. Here's that correlation on full display.

The company has developed at least a tenuous leadership position in a field that is still emerging, with many new competitors to challenge the established firms. Crypto exchanges also act as asset custodians and brokers, which is beyond what other types of securities exchanges do. COIN is also the custodian for most of the new crypto ETFs. Still, this is likely to be a highly cyclical industry, which corroborates my view that from time to time, CONY will be a very attractive ETF. But not just yet, in the form I desire to own it, with that put attached.

The "magic" has worn off of CONY, which might speed that along. This is one of the lowest 3-month returns it has posted since its 2023 inception.

The factor grades show a business that is nicely profitable, growing "like a weed" (not to be confused with cannabis stocks), but where the concept of "valuation" in the traditional sense has not yet come to be applied rigorously by many market participants. That's par for the course with a new and growing industry.

CONY: final thoughts for now

This is not a "sell" in that I think the stock's volatility will be disruptive, but not yet at the point where it looks like the underlying stock will roll over hard in the near term. But I'm not buying it here, thus a "hold" best describes my relative view on CONY. I'll bide my time on this member of the YieldMax family.

data.title: Nova: A New Star In The Making
data.description:
In September, I adopted a measured approach to shares of Nova Measurements (NASDAQ:NVMI) in which I praised the business for quadrupling sales over the past decade, accompanied by huge margin gains.

A mere $2 stock in the early 2000s was still trading at just $20 in 2019, after which huge momentum unleashed the stock to a high of $250 over the summer. Momentum has continued over the past half a year, rightfully sending shares continuously higher.

I greatly admire the business here, but am not willing to commit given the recent momentum, although I am very eager to keep a close eye on the shares.

An Advanced Semiconductor Player

Nova is an innovator and provider of dimensional materials and chemical metrology solutions, used in advanced process control within semiconductor manufacturing. These solutions are based on advanced technologies relating to optical scatterometry, x-ray fluorescence and photoelectron spectroscopy, among others.

The smaller chips get, the more process nodes are required, with capital investments per wafer exponentially on the increase as well. This is also driven by adoption of new technologies such as spectral interferometry, generative AI, raman spectroscopy and direct metal replenishment, among others.

These solutions have been key in advancing the goals of its semiconductor clients, as the company has quadrupled sales to over half a billion in just a decade's time, while operating margins tripled to about 30% of sales.

2023 was somewhat of a challenging year, as was the case for many, with sales reported down 9% to $518 million, with operating earnings down 12% to $132 million. This year of stagnation, however, followed a spectacular 37% increase in 2022 sales. GAAP earnings came in at $136 million, resulting in earning of just over $4 per share based on a share tally of 32 million shares.

Momentum Returns

In May, Nova reported a 6% increase in first quarter sales to $142 million, as momentum improved during the year. Second quarter sales rose as much as 28% to $157 million, while record bookings were reported, across geographies, customer accounts and technologies.

On this quarterly revenue number, GAAP earnings were reported at $45 million, equal to $1.41 per share. While this was impressive, it included $8 million in net interest income, equal to $0.27 per share. Adjusted for this, realistic earnings were trending at $4 and change per share.

Trading at $200 over the summer, operating assets were valued around $5.6 billion, equal to about 10 times sales given after factoring in substantial net cash holdings. Trading at 40 times realistic earnings, valuations were demanding, but this was backed up by real operating momentum.

This came after the company guided for third quarter sales between $168 and $176 million. Believing that the business traded around 8 times annualised sales and 35 times earnings (based on the third quarter guidance) I was aware of the track record and momentum, yet feared a dangerous set-up in case momentum would fade.

On Fire - Again

After shares declined during the fall, and traded around the $170 mark in November, shares have seen great momentum so far in 2025. After a relentless rally, shares trade at $268 per share, and that is even after shares are down twenty dollars from the highs.

The fall in November coincided with a 39% increase in reported third quarter sales, with revenues of $179 million coming in comfortably above the guided range. Moreover, momentum was set to continue, with fourth quarter sales seen between $181 and $191 million.

Later that month, Nova announced a $60 million bolt-on deal to acquire German-based Sentronics, a wafer metrology tools provider used for backend semiconductor manufacturing. This was really a bolt-on deal, with few details announced, other than that the deal was anticipated to be accretive to non-GAAP earnings per share upon closing.

By mid-February, Nova reported a 45% increase in fourth quarter sales to $195 million, again outpacing the guided range. Operating profits of $55 million could come in at $44 million (excluding net interest income), for earnings of $1.37 per share based on a share tally of 32 million shares (on a diluted basis). At this pace, earnings are trending close to $5.50 per share, that is excluding net interest income.

Growth Continues

While the company ended the year on a spectacular note, momentum is only set to continue into 2025. First quarter sales are seen between $205 and $215 million, with further advancements seen on the bottom line as well. This makes it very easy to see earnings trend at $6-7 per share (excluding interest income).

These cash holdings are very substantial at $811 million and will remain at three-quarter of a billion following the purchase of Sentronics, equal to $23 per share based on the diluted share count. This implies that operating assets trade at $245 per share, equal to 35-40 times earnings, based on earnings power of $6-7 per share.

While I am very impressed with the strong performance, I am mindful of 60% returns seen in just about three months time, as a $170 level (with the benefit of hindsight) looked like a great bargain in November.

At current levels I am erring to the cautious side, as I judge the situation to be somewhat similar to September, with current earnings multiples still being demanding after a period of momentum seen already. That said, the great growth trajectory and somewhat reasonable multiples, make that Nova could become an acquisition target as well in my view at some point in time.

At current levels I am very impressed by the business, yet mindful of the momentum seen already as well, making me take a wait-and-see-approach. This also comes in part as China is responsible for about 40% of sales, and the wider Asian region combined making up about 80% of sales.

I look forward with great interest to learn more on the business momentum in 2025, and to turn more upbeat if unexpected hiccups might be seen in the stock.

data.title: Norfolk Southern: From Setback To Comeback
data.description:
Introduction

Norfolk Southern (NYSE:NSC) embodies a recovery play that has recently outperformed the market. After the derailment in East Palestine, OH, the company faced significant costs that pressured its margins and tanked the stock. But, last July, I argued it could possibly be the best railroad investment in the short term. As a matter of fact, it beat all of its peers. The reason was foreseeable, alongside increasing intermodal traffic and rising demand for chemicals (one of the largest commodities NSC moves), the company was starting to report earnings against very easy comparables and was getting back on track to run its operations smoothly.

Well, as some may know, eight months ago, I happened to be a net seller of stocks as I was raising some cash for other needs. As a result, I didn't buy Norfolk Southern, and I still don't own it. In fact, right now, I have 5% of my portfolio concentrated in Canadian National (CNI), I don't own Union Pacific (UNP) anymore, and I only have a tiny fraction of my portfolio invested in Canadian Pacific (CP). This doesn't mean I am not bullish on the industry overall or that I am not interested in Norfolk Southern. Hence, it is about time to update my research after the idea that the stock would have offered a decent upside in the second half of 2024 played out. We have to answer two questions:

  1. How is Norfolk performing in a mixed environment?
  2. Has Norfolk caught up with its fair value, or is there still some upside left?

So, without further ado, let's take a look at the whole picture, starting with the latest earnings report and then collecting some data from the macroeconomic environment to understand what Norfolk's Q1 may look like.

Norfolk Southern's Q4 Earnings Review

Norfolk Southern reported revenues of $3B, which represented a 2% YoY decrease. The operating ratio in Q4 was 62.6%, which shows further improvement as the company strives to get the ratio back to below 60%. While in 2023, the Ohio incident brought over $1.1B in extra costs, this year, total incident-related expenses were down to $325M, significantly helping the company's margins and profits. As a result, Norfolk's operating expenses improved by 7%, and the operating margin decreased by 390bps YoY. In Q4, the reported operating income of $1.1B was 11% above Q4 2023. This proves that Norfolk was indeed going against easy comparables.

For the full year, Norfolk reported flat top-line growth, a 3% decrease in opex, a 160bps operating ratio improvement, a 5% growth in operating income, and an 11% growth in net income. A big help came from achieving nearly $300M in cost savings, beating the target of $250M.

Norfolk guided for 3% revenue growth and a 150bps OR improvement. It also announced it will resume its share repurchases starting in Q1 2025. Since early 2023, Norfolk has paid a steady $1.35 dividend to protect its balance sheet before the huge costs of the East Palestine incident. A few weeks ago, Norfolk declared another $1.35 quarterly dividend, but the path to delever the balance sheet has been taken, and we could then see a dividend hike in the second half of the year. But the fact that Norfolk is resuming its share repurchases restores part of its value creation framework. This is critical because its main competitor, CSX Corporation (CSX), has been able to keep reducing its outstanding shares and created a gap of almost 5pps in just less than two years.

Norfolk reports its freight revenues under three categories: merchandise, intermodal, and coal. This last one is the most important one for Norfolk since its rail network unfolds through the Appalachians, where coal is mined. While coals bears bad news and is expected to be down this year to due to lower natural gas prices, intermodal is expected to keep growing. Merchandise shows a mixed picture, with grain and chemicals performing well, while automotive and metals should be down vs. last year.

Does this outlook find support anywhere? Let's take a look.

Railroading in 2025

As the Association of American Railroads explains, "strong consumer demand drives strong intermodal movements," but "tariffs are at the forefront" of the public debate. This year, freight rail is slightly up YoY, with a +0.2% increase in January. The carloads mix shows coal (the largest carload commodity by volume) continues its decline, while grain and chemicals surged. Industrial products, which include cars but also crushed stone and gravel, are declining, reflecting a still soft industrial economy in the U.S.

Nonetheless, when chemicals perform well, we can expect an industrial rebound to take place soon. As a matter of fact, the ISM manufacturing PMI rose to 50.9%, finally crossing the critical threshold of 50%, which separates recession from expansion. This suggests cautious optimism for this year, although manufacturing rebounds are not quick processes, especially when interest rates are high, and many still expect a few cuts soon.

Norfolk has to release a weekly traffic report. If we look at the last one (week 7 ending February 9, 2025), we see that, so far this year, chemicals and grain are indeed growing YoY. But total merchandise carloads are actually down by over 10k loadings. However, intermodal is truly doing great, and it is helping the whole company report a total increase in carloadings. However, intermodal also has a lower revenue-per-carload metric. So, this mix might be a bit detrimental to Norfolk's top line.

However, from what we are seeing reported until now, the goal of 3% revenue growth seems to be reached by the end of the year. Some revenue growth will be helpful while the company improves its efficiency: it means more profits are coming in, and this will help Norfolk further protect its balance sheet.

Let's speak a bit about the threat posed by tariffs. Surely, intermodal will be hurt. But, in any case, whether goods are produced within the U.S. or outside, they will still need to be transported. As a result, I am not that concerned about this threat.

Valuation

We answered the first question. Now we tackle the second one: is Norfolk fairly valued or not? In a mixed environment, it is difficult to forecast explosive growth for the next few years. After all, this is not what investing in Class 1 railroads is about. This is a playground for LT-oriented investors who are willing to let these stocks compound while collecting growing dividends.

Right now, Norfolk trades at a fwd PE just below 20, which means it is less expensive than the market. Considering 2025 will be a year of EPS growth, the fwd PE ratio is currently graded with a B- by Seeking Alpha. The P/FCF, a very important ratio for railroads, is 15, but we also have to consider that Norfolk's capex will come a bit down in future years after it ends paying for the incident in Ohio. In 2026, analysts expect Norfolk's EPS to be $14.92, meaning the fwd PE is 17. More or less, the market expects Norfolk to keep growing between 3% to 6% per year while improving its efficiency and, thus, its EPS. If buybacks resume, too, it would not be absurd to expect Norfolk to grow its EPS above 10% per year. In this case, the PE ratio will be reduced by 1.5/2 points per year. Moreover, the dividend yield is now above 2%, and this is high for Norfolk's average.

In a market where almost everything is expensive, I think Norfolk still offers a decent opportunity, and this is why I keep rating it as a buy.

It is gross of any fee waivers or expense reimbursement. The net expense ratio is the expense ratio after the application of any waivers or reimbursement. This is the actual ratio that investors paid during the fund’s most recent fiscal year. Please see the prospectus for more information.

Returns or yields for the fund would have be lower if a portion of the management fee had not been waived. Review the semiannual or annual report for the most current information. Expense ratio is as of the fund's current prospectus. The I Class minimum investment amount is$5 million($3 million for endowments and foundations) per fund.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Market Review

Munis retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the quarter. However, municipal bonds(munis) generally fared better than the taxable fixed-income market. A third-quarter rally, aided by the September launch of the Federal Reserve’s easing campaign, helped most bond sectors, including munis, maintain one-year gains.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1%(annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved, and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Munis outperformed Treasuries. Muni yields tracked Treasury yields higher during the quarter, but at a slower pace, and most muni-to-Treasury ratios rose. Overall, munis outperformed Treasuries. Additionally, general obligation(GO) bonds fared better than revenue bonds, and shorter-maturity bonds generally outperformed longer-maturity issues.

Supply slowed in the quarter, grew for the year. As expected, fourth-quarter municipal bond supply shrunk compared with the third quarter’s level, as many issuers pushed deals forward to avoid presidential election-triggered volatility. For the year, total muni supply increased 29% compared with 2023, according to Barclays.

Fundamentals remained stable. While municipal credit trends continued to level off, balance sheets for most state and local entities remained fairly robust. Fiscal 2025 budgets for most U.S. states include modest revenue growth and conservative spending plans alongside strong reserve balances and liquidity. Additionally, rating upgrades slightly outpaced downgrades, and defaults remained rare.

High-yield munis outperformed. Muni credit spreads tightened modestly for the quarter. High-yield municipal bonds declined but outperformed investment-grade munis for the quarter. For the year, high-yield munis significantly outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Duration detracted. Our longer-than-index duration position weighed on the portfolio’s performance compared with the benchmark as yields rose. Our positioning along the yield curve, which favored short and intermediate maturities, helped offset some negative duration effects.

Security selection contributed. Security selection boosted results compared with the benchmark, largely due to our choices in the hospital, water and sewer and corporate muni sectors. These choices more than offset negative selection effects in the retirement community, special tax and multifamily housing sectors.

Sector allocations helped. Underweight positions relative to the benchmark in the prerefunded, other utility, state GO bond and multifamily housing sectors lifted results compared with the benchmark. These weightings more than offset the negative effects from overweight positions in the corporate muni and tobacco sectors and an underweight in local GO bonds. Additionally, our high-yield overweight modestly aided results.

Positioning for the Future

Growth may gather momentum. We expect slower growth in early 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, may weaken consumer demand and employment. By 2025’s second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may reignite growth. As the Trump administration's tax policy unfolds, we’ll monitor the potential muni market effects.

Credit backdrop to moderate. We expect previously strong credit trends to continue moderating. However, healthy reserves and sound liquidity should help mitigate the effects of slowing revenue growth and waning federal stimulus funds. We also expect an increase in state and local governments facing larger projected budget gaps, such as those challenging Chicago and Oakland, California.

Focused on quality. Given tight valuations and our expectations for growth to slow, we are maintaining a bias toward higher-quality securities and a slight duration overweight relative to the benchmark. As always, stringent security selection guides our process.

Valuations and fiscal policy uncertainties are keeping us focused on select sectors, waiting for better opportunities to boost credit exposure.
